rows.v: A close-range shot, usually banked softly off the backboard or laid over the rim, taken while moving toward the basket.
She drove the lane, absorbed the contact, and still kissed the layup off the glass — the most basic shot in the game, made to look effortless.
Descriptive — the shooter 'lays the ball up' gently toward the hoop. It's the first shot most players ever learn.
A field goal scored from behind the three-point arc, worth three points instead of two — the shot that reshaped modern basketball.
Down two with seconds left, he stepped back behind the line and let the three-pointer fly; the gym held its breath as it arced toward the rim.
The three-point line was adopted by the NBA in 1979 after experiments in the old ABA; decades later, analytics made it the centerpiece of strategy.
A two-player action where one sets a screen ('pick') for the ball-handler, then cuts ('rolls') to the basket for a pass.
They ran the pick-and-roll to perfection all night — the screen freeing the guard, the big man rolling to the rim, the defense always a step behind.
Descriptive of its two phases. Often called basketball's most fundamental play, it has anchored offenses from the playground to the pros for generations.
A pass thrown near the rim for a teammate to catch in mid-air and finish with a dunk or layup before landing.
The lob hung in the air for an impossible beat before he rose, caught it, and slammed the alley-oop home as the bench exploded.
From the French 'allez, hop!' — a cry used by circus acrobats and trapeze artists before a leap, borrowed for the airborne finish.
Clearing out a side of the court so one player can go one-on-one against a single defender, isolating the matchup.
Late in the clock they cleared out for an iso, handing the ball to their best scorer and letting four teammates simply watch the duel unfold.
Short for 'isolation,' the term grew as star-driven offenses leaned on individual scorers to beat their defender in open space.
Scoring by jumping and forcing the ball directly down through the hoop with one or both hands — basketball's most emphatic play.
He caught the lob and threw down a thunderous dunk, hanging on the rim a beat too long as the crowd lost its collective mind.
From 'dunk,' to dip something into liquid — the motion of pushing the ball down into the basket. It was actually banned in college play through the 1970s.
A dribble move that quickly switches the ball from one hand to the other to fake a defender out of position.
His crossover was lethal; one hard dribble left, then a snap back right, and the defender's ankles were left tangled on the hardwood.
Descriptive — the ball 'crosses over' between hands. The move's flashiest form, the 'ankle-breaker,' became an icon of 1990s playground and pro style.
A soft, high-arcing shot lofted over taller defenders near the basket before they can contest it. Also called a 'teardrop.'
Too small to challenge the giant in the paint, the guard floated a teardrop over his outstretched arms, the ball dropping in untouched.
Named for the way the ball seems to 'float' over the defense. It's the small player's classic answer to shot-blockers guarding the rim.
Establishing position with your back to the basket near the low post, using size and footwork to score close in.
The big man posted up on the block, backed his defender down with two dribbles, and finished with a smooth turnaround over his shoulder.
From 'the post,' the area near the basket. Once the dominant way big men scored, the post-up has faded somewhat in the three-point era.
A defensive scheme where each defender guards one specific opponent, following them across the court.
They switched to man-to-man in the fourth, every defender locked onto a man, turning the game into five individual battles of will.
Descriptive — each defender takes one man. It's the foundational defense taught before players learn the team-oriented complexities of zone.
A defensive scheme where players guard areas of the court rather than specific opponents, packing space to deter drives.
The 2-3 zone clogged the paint completely, forcing the offense into rushed jumpers all night and daring them to beat it from outside.
Descriptive — defenders cover 'zones' of the floor. The NBA actually banned zone defense for decades to keep the game flowing before reintroducing it in 2001.
Deflecting or stopping an opponent's shot in mid-air without fouling — one of defense's most dramatic plays. Also a 'rejection.'
He timed the leap perfectly and swatted the layup off the backboard, a rejection so emphatic the shooter just stood and stared.
From the general sense of 'to block,' to obstruct. The spectacular swat-into-the-stands version is showy; purists prize the controlled block that keeps possession.
Taking the ball away from the offense, by picking off a pass or knocking it loose from a dribbler.
She read the lazy cross-court pass, jumped the lane for the steal, and was gone the other way before anyone could react.
From 'to steal,' to take what isn't yours. It's the defensive playmaker's signature stat, the reward for anticipation and quick hands.
Using your body to seal off an opponent and carve out space for a rebound as a shot goes up.
He didn't grab a single highlight, but his relentless boxing out gave his guards the rebounds that quietly won the game.
From 'boxing out' an opponent — putting your body like a box between them and the ball. It's the unglamorous fundamental behind every rebound.
An aggressive defense applied across the entire court, pressuring the offense the moment they inbound to force turnovers.
Trailing late, they sprang the full-court press, swarming the inbounds pass and forcing a panicked turnover that swung the whole game.
Descriptive — the 'press' extends the full length of the court. Famously weaponized by coaches as a comeback tool and a way to exhaust opponents.
When a defender leaves their own assignment to assist a teammate who's been beaten, then recovers.
Beaten off the dribble, he trusted his teammate's help defense; sure enough, the weak-side big rotated over to wall off the rim.
Descriptive — a defender comes to 'help.' Good help defense is the hallmark of a connected team, turning five individuals into a single shifting wall.
The primary ball-handler and floor leader, responsible for running the offense and setting up teammates. The 'one.'
The point guard surveyed the floor like a chess player, calling the play, probing the defense, and slipping a no-look pass to the open shooter.
From 'point,' the position at the top of the offense from which a guard 'points' the team's attack. Traditionally the smallest, quickest player.
The painted rectangular area beneath each basket — also the 'key' or 'lane' — where much of the game's contact happens.
He owned the paint all night, swatting drives and grabbing rebounds; in that cramped, physical space, his size simply ruled.
Named because the area is literally painted a solid color on the court. The related 'key' refers to its old keyhole shape in earlier eras of the game.
The team's best substitute, first off the bench, often a scorer who provides an instant spark.
He embraced the sixth-man role, entering with the second unit and lighting up tired defenses with a burst of scoring off the bench.
Named because they are effectively the sixth starter — the first player off the bench beyond the five on the floor. The NBA gives a dedicated award for it.
Traditionally the tallest player, stationed near the basket to score inside, rebound, and protect the rim. The 'five.'
The center anchored everything, a towering presence who altered every shot in the paint and cleaned the glass at both ends.
Named for the position's traditional role at the 'center' of action near the hoop. The role has evolved as modern centers stretch out to shoot threes.
The three-point line itself — the curved boundary beyond which made shots count for three. Also 'beyond the arc.'
Spotting up beyond the arc, she caught and released in one motion; the analytics era has made that spot the most valuable real estate on the floor.
Named for its curved, arc-like shape on the court. 'From beyond the arc' has become shorthand for the entire three-point revolution in the modern game.
The boundary line running under each basket, along the ends of the court. Players use it as an extra 'defender' to trap dribblers.
She drove the baseline and reversed it under the hoop, using the line itself as cover before laying it in on the far side.
Descriptive — it forms the 'base' line at each end of the court. A defender who steers a dribbler toward it can trap them with nowhere to go.
Pushing the ball up the floor quickly after a rebound or steal to score before the defense can set up.
The rebound triggered a fast break, three players streaking downcourt in a blur, the outlet pass finding the leader for an easy two.
Descriptive — a 'fast' attack that 'breaks' away from the defense before it recovers. The thrilling antidote to slow, set half-court basketball.
A balanced stance, ball in hand, from which a player can immediately shoot, pass, or dribble — keeping the defense guessing.
He caught the ball in triple-threat position, and the defender froze, unable to commit because all three options were equally live.
Named for the three simultaneous 'threats' — shoot, pass, drive — the stance preserves. It's a cornerstone of fundamental offensive teaching.
A legal block set by an offensive player to free a teammate from their defender. The same action as a 'pick.'
The screen caught the defender flush, and for a split second the shooter was wide open — all the daylight a great shooter ever needs.
From 'to screen,' to shield or block from view — the screener's body shields the teammate from the defender. 'Pick' is the more informal synonym.
Passing to a teammate and immediately cutting to the basket to receive a return pass for an easy score.
Classic give-and-go: he hit the post, cut hard to the rim, and the ball came right back for a layup before the defense even turned its head.
Descriptive of its rhythm — you 'give' the ball, then 'go' to the basket. One of the oldest two-man plays in the sport, predating the modern game.
Sending two defenders to guard a single ball-handler, usually to trap a star scorer or force the ball out of their hands.
They double-teamed him the instant he touched the ball, two defenders swarming, betting that anyone else beating them was a risk worth taking.
Descriptive — two defenders 'team up' on one. The strategic gamble is that leaving someone else open is the lesser evil against an elite scorer.
The phase when possession changes and a team moves quickly from defense to offense, before the opposing defense is set.
They were lethal in transition, turning every stop into a sprint the other way, scoring before the defense could even find its assignments.
From 'transition,' the change from one state to another — here, from defense to offense. Modern pace-and-space basketball thrives in these chaotic moments.
A badly missed shot that clangs hard off the rim or backboard, as ugly and heavy as a thrown brick.
He threw up an air-balling brick at the worst possible moment, and the home crowd let him hear about it with a chorus of groans.
Slang likening a clanging miss to a brick — heavy, hard, and graceless. The crowd chant 'brick! brick!' to rattle a struggling free-throw shooter is a staple.
Being fouled while making a shot, earning the basket plus one free throw — a three-point play from a two-point bucket.
He finished through the contact and roared 'and-one!' as the whistle blew, the chance to complete a three-point play already in his eyes.
Short for 'the basket and one free throw.' The triumphant shout has become a celebration in itself, even lending its name to a streetball brand.
Slang for a long three-point shot, as if launched from far away — 'from downtown.'
He pulled up from well beyond the line and drained it — 'from downtown!' the announcer howled, the call as old as the three itself.
Broadcaster slang evoking a shot from way out, 'downtown,' far from the basket. It became a signature call of the three-point era's growing showmanship.
To dunk dramatically over a defender, humiliating them so memorably the moment belongs on a poster.
He rose over the helpless defender and posterized him with a one-handed slam, an image destined for every highlight reel that night.
From the literal posters such dunks became — the dunker immortalized, the defender frozen beneath. A modern coinage of the highlight-and-social-media age.
A shot released just before the game or quarter clock expires that counts if it leaves the hand in time — the ultimate dramatic finish.
The buzzer-beater banked in as time hit zero, and the arena erupted; one shot, half a second, and a season changed forever.
Named for beating the 'buzzer' that signals the end of a period. Replay review now settles the agonizing question of whether the ball left the hand in time.
Recording double-digit totals in three statistical categories in one game — usually points, rebounds, and assists.
He notched a triple-double by the third quarter — points, boards, and dimes all in double figures — a stat line that screams all-around dominance.
Descriptive — three categories reaching 'double' digits. The rarer 'quadruple-double' adds a fourth, a feat achieved only a handful of times in NBA history.
A perfect made shot that drops cleanly through the net without touching the rim — pure and satisfying.
Nothing but net — the swish so clean the net snapped up and the shooter was already backpedaling on defense, certain it was good.
Onomatopoeic, imitating the soft 'swish' sound of the ball passing through the net untouched. The sound itself has become shorthand for a perfect shot.
